subtopic_detail: Sentinel facial features in foetal alcohol spectrum disorder assessment
stem: You are the paediatric registrar in a community clinic reviewing a 4-year-old boy referred by his kindergarten teacher for behavioural concerns and slow language. His birth mother has been open about ongoing alcohol use through the pregnancy. He is in long-term foster care. His foster mother reports impulsivity, poor emotion regulation, and difficulty following two-step instructions. Growth is below the 3rd centile for weight and length. There is no history of perinatal infection, head trauma, or seizures, and metabolic screening done at school entry was normal.

data_table.Growth: Weight 13.1 kg (<3rd centile), height 95 cm (<3rd centile), head circumference 47.5 cm (<3rd centile)
data_table.Facial findings: Short palpebral fissures, smooth philtrum, thin upper vermilion border
data_table.Development: Receptive language 2 years 6 months, expressive language 2 years, gross motor age-appropriate
data_table.Hearing and vision: Audiology and ophthalmology screening normal in the last 6 months

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in confirming the suspected diagnosis?

options.text: Send a chromosomal microarray and fragile X testing as the next investigation
options.rationale: Genetic testing is reserved for atypical features, additional dysmorphism, or when other syndromes are competing on the differential; the sentinel triad with confirmed prenatal alcohol exposure makes broad genetic testing low yield as the first step. (Source: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD)
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

options.text: Refer for multidisciplinary neurodevelopmental assessment with standardised facial measurement
options.rationale: Australian diagnostic criteria require multidisciplinary assessment confirming three sentinel facial features and severe impairment in three or more neurodevelopmental domains, alongside documented prenatal exposure. (Source: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD)
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

options.text: Commence a 6-week trial of methylphenidate for presumed attentional and impulse difficulties
options.rationale: Stimulant trials may be considered after formal diagnosis and behavioural strategies have failed, not as a diagnostic shortcut in a 4-year-old with developmental concerns. (Source: RCH CPG: ADHD)

options.text: Arrange brain MRI to exclude a structural cause of the developmental delay
options.rationale: Routine MRI is not part of the diagnostic pathway when the clinical picture and prenatal exposure are typical; imaging is reserved for focal neurology or seizures. (Source: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD)
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

options.text: Reassure the family and re-review developmental progress in 6 months in clinic
options.rationale: Delaying assessment misses the early-intervention window that improves long-term outcomes when neurodevelopmental impairment is already evident. (Source: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD)
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

explanation.summary: Foetal alcohol spectrum disorder is diagnosed in Australia using the Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD, which requires either three sentinel facial features (short palpebral fissures, smooth philtrum, thin upper lip vermilion) with confirmed or unknown prenatal alcohol exposure, OR severe neurodevelopmental impairment across three of ten domains with confirmed exposure. Multidisciplinary assessment is mandated. Early identification opens access to NDIS support, structured behavioural therapy, and educational accommodations. Stimulants may help comorbid attentional symptoms after diagnosis but are not the first response.
key_points: Three sentinel facial features plus exposure suffice for diagnosis of FASD with three sentinel features
Without all three facial features, severe impairment in 3 of 10 neurodevelopmental domains plus confirmed exposure is required
Growth restriction is supportive but not required for diagnosis
Multidisciplinary assessment is mandatory in the Australian framework
Early diagnosis unlocks NDIS and educational supports
explanation.pearls: Smooth philtrum and thin upper vermilion are scored against the University of Washington lip-philtrum guide, with rank 4 or 5 counting as a sentinel feature.

sources.label: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

subtopic_detail: Diagnostic pathway for suspected Hirschsprung disease in delayed meconium passage
stem: You are the paediatric registrar called to assess a term male newborn on day 3 who has not passed meconium since birth and now has bilious vomiting after each feed. He was born by uncomplicated vaginal birth at 39 weeks. His abdomen is progressively distended, and a digital rectal examination produces a forceful expulsion of meconium and gas. He has not had any prior abdominal surgery. There is no family history of cystic fibrosis, and the newborn screening blood spot is pending.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9, HR 158, RR 52, SpO2 98 percent on room air
data_table.Examination: Distended soft non-tender abdomen, visible bowel loops, no inguinal hernia, anus normally sited and patent
data_table.Hydration: Capillary refill 2 seconds, mucous membranes moist
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.36, lactate 2.1 mmol/L; supine abdominal radiograph shows dilated bowel loops with paucity of rectal gas

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

options.text: Proceed directly to laparotomy and intraoperative levelling biopsies under anaesthesia
options.rationale: Surgery is the definitive treatment after histological confirmation; proceeding without a confirmed diagnosis risks unnecessary laparotomy in alternative causes such as meconium plug or ileal atresia. (Source: RCH CPG: Hirschsprung disease)

options.text: Request a water-soluble contrast enema to characterise the transition zone in colonic anatomy
options.rationale: Contrast enema is supportive but neonates often lack a mature transition zone; its sensitivity in the first weeks is limited and it does not establish histological diagnosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Hirschsprung disease)

options.text: Arrange a suction rectal biopsy for histological assessment of ganglion cells
options.rationale: Suction rectal biopsy showing absence of ganglion cells with hypertrophic nerve trunks is the definitive diagnostic test in neonates and can be done cot-side without anaesthesia. (Source: RCH CPG: Hirschsprung disease)

options.text: Trial high-dose oral lactulose and review the response over the next 48 hours
options.rationale: Stool softeners do not address aganglionic obstruction and risk worsening proximal distension with enterocolitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Hirschsprung disease)

options.text: Send sweat chloride testing to exclude meconium ileus from cystic fibrosis
options.rationale: Sweat testing is unreliable before 2 weeks of age and the explosive release of stool on rectal examination is classic for Hirschsprung rather than meconium ileus. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic fibrosis)

explanation.summary: Hirschsprung disease presents in approximately 90 percent within the neonatal period with delayed meconium beyond 48 hours, abdominal distension and bilious vomiting. The classic squirt sign on rectal examination is highly suggestive. Suction rectal biopsy is the diagnostic gold standard. Contrast enema may identify a transition zone but is unreliable in neonates because the aganglionic segment has not yet hypertrophied proximally. Enterocolitis is the most dangerous early complication and warrants empiric antibiotics and rectal washouts.
key_points: Failure to pass meconium within 48 hours is a red flag
Suction rectal biopsy is the diagnostic test of choice
Histology shows absent ganglion cells with hypertrophic nerve trunks
Contrast enema sensitivity is limited in the neonatal period
Enterocolitis is the leading cause of mortality before surgery
explanation.pearls: Rectal biopsies must be taken at least 2 cm above the dentate line to avoid the physiologically hypoganglionic zone and a false-positive result.
